title: Are AI trading agents actually usable in live markets, or still mostly experimental?
author: Woodpecker5987
selftext:
People keep acting like all AI agents are basically the same, but once you actually try them, the differences are obvious, heres some ive noticed;

**AutoGPT:**  
Fun to experiment with, but still feels unpredictable. I wouldn’t trust it for anything involving real money.

**OpenClaw:**  
Powerful, no doubt. It can actually execute tasks, but setup + configuration is not beginner-friendly. You’re basically managing the agent as much as it’s helping you.

**GetClaw**:  
This one feels more practical for trading. No setup, runs immediately, and it’s always monitoring + ready to act. It’s less about building the system and more about actually using it.

That said, i believe AI trading is NOT passive income.

Even with GetClaw, you’re still:

testing prompts  
refining strategy  
managing risk  
reacting to market changes

The agent just executes faster and more consistently than you can.

I’ll be preparing for the Bitget AIagent tradingcompet using GetClaw mainly because I can iterate on strategies quickly without dealing with setup. But honestly, most of my time right now is going into figuring out solid prompts, not “letting AI trade.”

If this was truly passive, everyone using the same tools would be winning.

They’re not.

AI agents are leverage, not autopilot imo, so just a curious question here, has anyone here actually gotten consistent results with AI trading, or is everyone still tweaking setups?

comments.author: Intelligent-Mess71
comments.body:
Big picture, AI agents are just execution, not edge. If the strategy isn’t solid, they’ll just lose faster.

For example, if market conditions shift and the rules don’t adapt, the agent keeps firing bad trades.

Most people I’ve seen are still tweaking rules to control overtrading and behavior.

Feels more experimental than reliable right now. Are you running it fully auto or with oversight?

replies.author: Woodpecker5987
replies.body: could be an edge if they are executed right though. its not rigid but flexibile thats why i use prompt based on current market directions and getclaw executes what i want... i think you are right about it being experimental, but so far it has been 60-75% reliable for me
